examples.title: Murphy's Hardware
examples.subtitle: Books of First Entry — January 2026
examples.scenario: Murphy's Hardware is a sole trader business selling hardware supplies. All goods are subject to VAT at 23%. The following transactions occurred in January 2026. Enter each transaction into the correct book of first entry, calculating Gross = Net + VAT.

transactions.detail: Credit sale to Kelly Ltd — net €2,000 + VAT @ 23% — Invoice 101
Credit sale to Walsh Ltd — net €1,500 + VAT @ 23% — Invoice 102
Credit purchase from O'Brien Supplies — net €800 + VAT @ 23% — Invoice S001
Credit purchase from Cork Traders — net €1,200 + VAT @ 23% — Invoice S002
Issued credit note CN01 to Kelly Ltd — net €200 + VAT @ 23% — goods returned by customer
Received credit note CN-S01 from O'Brien Supplies — net €100 + VAT @ 23% — goods returned to supplier

tasks.title: Task 1 — Sales Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transactions 1 and 2 in the Sales Day Book. Gross = Net + VAT.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Sales Returns Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transaction 5 (credit note issued to Kelly Ltd) in the Sales Returns Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 3 — Purchases Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transactions 3 and 4 in the Purchases Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 4 — Purchases Returns Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transaction 6 (credit note received from O'Brien Supplies) in the Purchases Returns Day Book.

examples.title: Shannon Bakery
examples.subtitle: Cash Book — February 2026
examples.scenario:
Shannon Bakery operates a bank cash book. All receipts are lodged to the bank; all payments are made by cheque or direct debit. Opening bank balance 1 February 2026: €4,200 Dr.

Enter the following February transactions into the Cash Book Receipts and Payments columns. Include the opening balance in the Receipts side.

transactions.detail: Opening balance (Balance b/d) — €4,200 Dr — enters Receipts side

tasks.title: Task 1 — Cash Book
tasks.instruction: Complete the Cash Book — Receipts (transactions 1, 2, 3, 7) on the Dr side and Payments (transactions 4, 5, 6) on the Cr side. Enter the Bank (€) amount for each. Closing balance of Receipts minus Payments = €6,617.

examples.title: Galway Gift Shop
examples.subtitle: Trial Balance — 31 January 2026
examples.scenario:
Galway Gift Shop is a sole trader. The following account balances have been extracted from the ledgers at 31 January 2026. Extract a Trial Balance and verify that it balances (total debits = total credits).

Tip: Assets and Expenses → Debit side. Liabilities, Capital, and Income → Credit side.

tasks.title: Task 1 — Trial Balance
tasks.instruction: Enter all 12 accounts into the Trial Balance below. Place each balance on the correct side (Debit or Credit). The totals should both equal €42,400.

examples.title: Limerick Logistics
examples.subtitle: Trial Balance with Difference — Suspense Account
examples.scenario:
Limerick Logistics has extracted the following account balances at 31 March 2026. When totalled, the Trial Balance does not balance. A Suspense Account must be opened to park the difference while the error is investigated.

Then the error is found: the Purchases Day Book was undercast (totalled incorrectly) by €100. Write the correcting journal entry to close the Suspense account.

transactions.detail: Note: Dr total = €31,200 — Cr total = €31,300 — difference = €100 Cr > Dr

tasks.title: Task 1 — Trial Balance (with Suspense)
tasks.instruction: Enter all 9 accounts plus a Suspense Account to make the TB balance. Since Credit > Debit by €100, the Suspense goes on the DEBIT side (Dr Suspense €100). Both totals should equal €31,300.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Correcting Journal Entry
tasks.instruction: The error has been found: the Purchases Day Book total was undercast by €100 (posted as €11,900 instead of €12,000). Write the correcting journal entry to increase Purchases by €100 and close the Suspense account. Dr Purchases €100, Cr Suspense €100.

examples.title: Waterford Wholesale
examples.subtitle: Bank Reconciliation — January 2026
examples.scenario:
Waterford Wholesale has a Cash Book balance of €6,800 Dr at 31 January 2026. On comparing with the bank statement the following differences were identified:

  • Bank charges €75 — on bank statement, not in Cash Book
  • Direct debit — Insurance €250 — on bank statement, not in Cash Book
  • Unpresented cheque Chq 315 — €1,200 (in CB, not on statement)
  • Unpresented cheque Chq 316 — €600 (in CB, not on statement)
  • Outstanding lodgement — €600 (in CB, not on statement)
  • Bank statement balance: €5,275 Cr

tasks.title: Task 1 — Updated Cash Book
tasks.instruction:
Step 1: Update the Cash Book by deducting items on the bank statement that are not yet in the CB (bank charges and direct debit). The updated CB balance should be €6,475.

Step 2: Prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statement starting from the bank statement balance of €5,275. Add unpresented cheques and deduct outstanding lodgements to reconcile to the updated CB balance of €6,475.

examples.title: Dublin Deli
examples.subtitle: VAT Return — Quarter 1 2026
examples.scenario:
Dublin Deli is VAT-registered. The following sales and purchases occurred in Quarter 1 2026 (January–March). Calculate the VAT on each line and complete the VAT Return boxes T1–T5.

T1 = Total Sales (excl. VAT) · T2 = Total Purchases (excl. VAT) · T3 = Output VAT · T4 = Input VAT · T5 = VAT Payable (T3 – T4)

tasks.title: Task 1 — VAT Return
tasks.instruction: Enter the VAT for each sales and purchases category. Calculate VAT = Net × Rate. T3 (Output VAT) = sum of all sales VAT. T4 (Input VAT) = sum of all purchases VAT. T5 = T3 − T4.

salesRows.category: T1 — Total Sales / T3 — Output VAT

purchasesRows.category: T2 — Total Purchases / T4 — Input VAT

examples.title: Cork Confectionery
examples.subtitle: Error Correction — Journal Entries
examples.scenario: Cork Confectionery has identified the following errors in its bookkeeping records. For each error, write the correcting journal entry (Dr and Cr). Leave the Date column blank if not specified.

transactions.detail: Error of Commission: Stationery €120 was posted to Motor Expenses account. Both are expense accounts — correct by moving it to Stationery.
Error of Omission: Sales invoice to Ryan Ltd — net €1,000 + VAT €230 (23%), gross €1,230 — was completely omitted from the books.
Error of Principle: Purchase of a computer €2,500 was posted to the Purchases account. It should be Equipment (a fixed asset).
Complete Reversal: Wages €1,800 were posted as Dr Bank €1,800, Cr Wages €1,800 — the debit and credit are reversed.

tasks.title: Task 1 — Correct Error 1 (Commission)
tasks.instruction: Stationery €120 was posted to Motor Expenses. Both are expenses so this is an Error of Commission. Move the amount to the correct account.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Correct Error 2 (Omission)
tasks.instruction: The sale to Ryan Ltd (€1,230 gross = €1,000 net + €230 VAT) was never recorded. Enter it now.

tasks.title: Task 3 — Correct Error 3 (Principle)
tasks.instruction: A computer €2,500 was charged to Purchases. Purchases is for trading stock (expense); a computer is a fixed asset (Equipment). This is an Error of Principle.

tasks.title: Task 4 — Correct Error 4 (Complete Reversal)
tasks.instruction: Wages €1,800 were posted Dr Bank, Cr Wages — completely reversed. To fix a complete reversal, double the amount: Dr the correct account, Cr the correct account, both for 2× the original amount (€3,600).

examples.title: Athlone Architects
examples.subtitle: Suspense Account & Correction — March 2026
examples.scenario: Athlone Architects has extracted its Trial Balance at 31 March 2026 but the columns do not agree. The credit side exceeds the debit side by €100. A Suspense Account is opened on the debit side. Later, the error is found: the Sales account was overcast by €100 (total was €12,100, should be €12,000). Write the correcting entry.

transactions.detail: Dr total = €32,000 · Cr total = €32,200 · Difference = €200 Cr > Dr

tasks.title: Task 1 — Trial Balance (with Suspense)
tasks.instruction: Enter all accounts in the Trial Balance. The Cr side exceeds Dr by €200. Open a Suspense Account on the Debit side for €200. Both totals should then equal €32,200.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Correcting Entry
tasks.instruction: Sales was overcast by €100 (posted as €12,100 instead of €12,000). To correct: Dr Sales €100 (reduce Sales), Cr Suspense €100 (close the suspense). The remaining €100 difference in the suspense is a separate issue — assume only this error applies to this entry.

rows.detail: Dr Sales (correction of overcast — reduce by €100)

examples.title: Kilkenny Kitchens
examples.subtitle: Comprehensive — Daybooks, Cash Book & Bank Rec
examples.scenario:
Kilkenny Kitchens is a kitchen fitting company. Opening bank balance 1 March 2026: €3,000 Dr. All VAT at 23%. Complete the Sales Day Book, Purchases Day Book, Cash Book, and Bank Reconciliation Statement for March 2026.

On reconciliation: Bank charges €50 were on the statement but not in the Cash Book. Cheque 403 (€300 petty cash) is in the CB but not yet presented at the bank. Bank statement balance = €3,279 Cr.

transactions.detail: Credit sale to Brady Ltd — net €3,000 + 23% VAT = gross €3,690 — Invoice 301
Credit sale to Nolan Ltd — net €2,500 + 23% VAT = gross €3,075 — Invoice 302
Credit purchase from Walsh Suppliers — net €4,000 + 23% VAT = gross €4,920 — Invoice W201
Cash sales lodged to bank — gross €984 (net €800 + VAT €184 @ 23%)
Paid Walsh Suppliers by cheque Chq 401 — €4,920
Issued cheque Chq 403 for petty cash — €300

tasks.title: Task 1 — Sales Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transactions 1 and 2 (credit sales to Brady Ltd and Nolan Ltd) in the Sales Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Purchases Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transaction 3 (credit purchase from Walsh Suppliers) in the Purchases Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 3 — Cash Book
tasks.instruction: Enter all receipts (opening balance, cash sales, Brady Ltd, Nolan Ltd) and payments (Walsh Suppliers, Wages, Petty Cash). Cash Book closing balance = €3,029.

tasks.title: Task 4 — Bank Reconciliation
tasks.instruction:
Step 1: Update the Cash Book — deduct bank charges €50 (on statement, not in CB) to get Updated CB = €2,979.

Step 2: BRS — Start with bank statement balance €3,279. Deduct unpresented Chq 403 (€300). Result must equal Updated CB (€2,979).

examples.title: Dublin Premium Events
examples.subtitle: Full Comprehensive — All Learning Outcomes
examples.scenario:
Dublin Premium Events Ltd provides event management services. Opening bank balance 1 January 2026: €12,000 Dr. VAT at 23% throughout. Complete all tasks for Q1 2026 (January–March).

On reconciliation: Bank charges €120 (not in CB). Chq 603 — Office supplies €500 — in CB but not on bank statement. Bank statement balance = €12,685 Cr.

Error: Invoice 501 to Hennessy Events (gross €6,150) was credited to Purchases instead of Sales. The debtor entry was correct.

transactions.detail: Credit sale — Hennessy Events — net €5,000 + VAT €1,150 = gross €6,150 — Invoice 501
Credit sale — Burke Catering — net €2,500 + VAT €575 = gross €3,075 — Invoice 502
Credit purchase — Sound Systems Ltd — net €3,500 + VAT €805 = gross €4,305 — Invoice P301
Credit sale — Conway Ltd — net €1,800 + VAT €414 = gross €2,214 — Invoice 503
Office supplies — Cheque 603 — €500 (in CB, not yet on bank statement)

tasks.title: Task 1 — Sales Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter the three credit sales (transactions 1, 2, 8) in the Sales Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 2 — Purchases Day Book
tasks.instruction: Enter transaction 3 (credit purchase from Sound Systems Ltd) in the Purchases Day Book.

tasks.title: Task 3 — Cash Book
tasks.instruction: Enter all receipts (opening balance €12,000, cash sales €2,460, Hennessy €6,150) and all payments (Sound Systems €4,305, Wages €3,500, Office Supplies Chq 603 €500). CB closing balance = €12,305.

tasks.title: Task 4 — Bank Reconciliation
tasks.instruction:
Update CB: deduct bank charges €120 → Updated CB = €12,185.
BRS: Bank statement €12,685 less unpresented Chq 603 €500 = €12,185 ✓

tasks.title: Task 5 — VAT Return
tasks.instruction: Complete the VAT Return for Q1 2026. Output VAT = 1,150 + 575 + 460 + 414 = €2,599. Input VAT = €805. T5 = €2,599 − €805 = €1,794 payable.

salesRows.category: T1 — Total Sales / T3 — Output VAT

purchasesRows.category: T2 — Total Purchases / T4 — Input VAT

tasks.title: Task 6 — Error Correction
tasks.instruction: Invoice 501 to Hennessy Events was credited to Purchases instead of Sales. This is an Error of Principle (Sales = income, Purchases = expense — different class). Correct by: Dr Purchases €5,000, Cr Sales €5,000. (VAT and the debtor were posted correctly.)

rows.detail: Dr Purchases (Inv 501 incorrectly posted to Purchases)
Cr Sales (correct account — error of principle)
